sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Diphtheria is a serious, potentially fatal infection caused by toxin-producing *Corynebacterium diphtheriae*. The toxin causes local tissue necrosis (forming a characteristic pseudo membrane) and systemic complications including myocarditis and neuropathy. Transmission occurs via respiratory droplets or direct contact with respiratory secretions or wound exudates and fluid from skin lesions.

- **Incubation:** 2-5 days (up to 10 days)
- **Infectious period:** A week before onset of symptoms and up to 6 weeks
- **Case fatality rate:** 5-10% even with treatment

**MANDATORY NOTIFIABLE DISEASE in Western Australia**

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
**Hallmark Sign:** Grey/white **pseudo membrane** on tonsils/pharynx. Leathery, tough, adherent. **Bleeds when you try to wipe it off.** May extend onto soft palate, uvula, or larynx.

**Early Symptoms (Days 1-2)**
- Mild sore throat - often less painful than it looks
- Low-grade fever 38-38.5°C - **patient looks sicker than the temperature suggests**
- Fatigue, malaise, loss of appetite
- Muffled or "hot potato" voice

**Progressing Disease (Days 2-4)**
- **Bull neck** - dramatic swelling from lymph nodes and tissue oedema
- Hoarse voice, barking cough (laryngeal involvement)
- Nasal discharge, possibly blood-tinged
- Dysphagia, drooling

**Severe / Late Complications**
| Complication | Timing | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Airway obstruction | Days 2-5 | Membrane extension and oedema |
| Myocarditis | Approximately 1 week | Arrhythmias, conduction defects, cardiomyopathy |
| Neuropathy | Weeks to months | Cranial nerve palsy, respiratory muscle weakness |

**DANGER SIGNS - ACT IMMEDIATELY:** 
- Stridor
- Respiratory distress
- Airway Obstruction
- Cyanosis
- Drooling/inability to swallow
- Arrhythmia/chest pain

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- **PPE:** Standard, contact and droplet precautions (surgical mask, eye protection, gloves, gown)
- **DO NOT attempt to remove or dislodge the pseudo membrane** - risk of haemorrhage and complete airway obstruction
- **Penicillin resistance** emerging in Australian outbreak strains (NT, QLD) - **azithromycin is preferred first-line (WHO 2024)**
- Inadequately vaccinated persons are at significantly higher risk of severe disease
- Cutaneous diphtheria can transmit to susceptible persons and cause respiratory disease in contacts
- Remote Indigenous community context due to possible vaccination gaps
- Overseas-born workers from endemic regions
- DAT has limited national supply so early coordination with WA Health and RFDS is essential

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
**Immediate Actions - On Suspicion**

- **ISOLATE:** Droplet precautions. Single room. Mask the patient.
- **CALL RFDS and WA HEALTH - REPORT NOW:** Mandatory notifiable on clinical suspicion. Contact your local Public Health Unit for urgent medical advice.
- Take swabs from tonsils prior to treatment where possible - do not delay treatment.
- **START ANTIBIOTICS:** Do not wait for laboratory confirmation.
- **AIRWAY READY: DO NOT REMOVE MEMBRANE**
- **Request antitoxin on evacuation aircraft**

ESOs MUST have an extremely low threshold for getting help (MESO sites call 000 where appropriate).

**Primary Survey**
- Position of comfort for airway protection
- Consider oxygen; titrate to SpO2 94% or above
- Have suction, airway adjuncts, and BVM ready at all times
- Vital sign survey - particularly respiratory rate, heart rate, blood pressure, SpO2, temperature, BGL dip
- Limb-lead ECG; continuous cardiac monitoring if available

**Supportive Care**
- Nil by mouth if airway concerns
- Anticipate fluid requirements; strict fluid balance
- Keep patient warm
- Analgesia/antipyretics as required
- Consider antiemetic if required

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
Ensure adrenaline and resuscitation equipment are immediately available.

**Airway Management**
- Anticipate airway deterioration - membrane can extend causing complete obstruction
- Prepare difficult airway equipment
- If intubation required, use smallest ETT that will pass - membrane narrows the lumen
- Surgical airway may be needed if membrane prevents intubation
- Avoid blind suctioning of pharynx (may dislodge membrane)
- Consider advanced airway early if stridor, respiratory distress, or declining GCS
- **MUST contact RFDS if advanced airway is required**

**Cardiac Monitoring**
- Continuous ECG monitoring for arrhythmias, conduction defects
- 12-lead ECG - look for PR prolongation, ST changes, T-wave inversion, heart block

**Supportive Care**
- Gain IV access, 2 x large bore
- Consider troponin (i-STAT) if available and time permits
- Consider blood draw for i-STAT (Chem8+, CG4+) where applicable
- Consider IV fluids where indicated
- Consider antiemetic
- Administer IV appropriate pain relief

**Antibiotics (in consult with on-call doctor)**
- **Azithromycin (preferred)**
- Erythromycin
- If allergy/contraindication to macrolides, consider doxycycline (200 mg day 1, then 100 mg BD for 7 days) in consultation with on-call doctor

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- **Swab Collection:** Specify "culture for suspected diphtheria" on request form - standard lab processing may not identify *C. diphtheriae* without specific request. Collect specimens before antibiotics where possible, but do not delay antibiotics for swabs. Always follow WA Department of Health guidance.
- Diphtheria does not reliably confer immunity - vaccinate during convalescence.
- All suspected cases of respiratory diphtheria must be evacuated off site.
